lines.text: SEGMENT 14 — Resistance and the quasispecies
Reverse transcriptase has no proofreading exonuclease.
It makes roughly one error per ten thousand to one hundred thousand bases incorporated.
The HIV genome is about 10,000 bases long.
So a single round of replication produces, on average, about one mutation per genome.
Some of those mutations are silent, some are deleterious, some have no effect, but a small fraction are advantageous.
In an untreated HIV-infected person, the virus produces roughly ten billion new virions per day.
Multiply that by one mutation per genome, and you generate, every day, roughly ten billion point mutations — which is far more than the size of the HIV genome itself.
In other words, every possible single-base mutation in the HIV genome is generated, somewhere, in that person, every single day.
And every possible double mutation arises every few weeks.
That has a profound implication.
The viral population inside an infected person is not a single genome with a few minor variants.
It is a quasispecies — a cloud of related but distinct genomes, all the variants that haven't yet been selected against by the immune system or by drugs, hovering around a consensus sequence.
Now think about drug pressure.
Give a patient a single drug.
Maybe ten million of the virions in their body, that day, already carry a mutation that confers some resistance to that drug.
Those variants survive.
They are selected for.
Within weeks, the drug stops working.
Monotherapy fails.
This was learned bitterly with AZT in the late 1980s, when six months of zidovudine monotherapy was followed by viral rebound carrying multiple AZT resistance mutations.
Now give two drugs from different classes.
The chance that a single virion already carries resistance mutations to both drugs is the product of the individual probabilities — say, 10^-5 × 10^-5 = 10^-10.
In ten billion virions per day you'd expect about one such variant.
Marginal.
With three drugs, 10^-15 — essentially never.

Triple combination therapy — HAART as it was originally called, now cART for combination antiretroviral therapy — works because the probability of pre-existing resistance to three independent drugs simultaneously is too small to find in a typical viral population.
This is why HIV therapy, from the late 1990s onward, has always been at least three drugs.
It is also why adherence matters so enormously.
Skip doses, drug levels drop, partial selection pressure favors variants with some resistance.
Skip enough doses and you essentially re-create monotherapy on a population that is now genetically diverse.
The major resistance mutations are worth knowing because they appear on real clinical resistance reports.

M184V is the signature lamivudine and emtricitabine mutation — it abolishes activity of those drugs and reduces fitness of the virus modestly, which is why some clinicians actually accept M184V on board as a way to keep the virus partially crippled.
K103N is the classic first-generation NNRTI mutation — it confers resistance to efavirenz and nevirapine with essentially no fitness cost.
K65R affects tenofovir.
Q151M and the thymidine analog mutations like T215Y affect zidovudine and other older NRTIs.
N155H and Q148K affect integrase inhibitors.
You don't have to memorize that list.

What you have to know is the underlying principle: high error rate, huge viral population, every mutation generated every day, combination therapy required to make the math work, adherence is everything.
That principle generalizes.
The same logic applies, with different numbers, to influenza, hepatitis C, SARS-CoV-2, tuberculosis.
Whenever you treat a rapidly evolving infectious agent, you treat it with multiple drugs that hit different targets.
Single-target monotherapy of a high-mutation-rate pathogen is, mathematically, almost always going to fail.
